learning_objectives: Identify the primary areas of law affecting digital communications professionals
Distinguish between laws that restrict communication and laws that protect communicators
Evaluate how the First Amendment applies to commercial and organizational speech
readings: Pember & Calvert, Mass Media Law, Ch. 1-3
EFF, 'A Legal Guide to Digital Communications'

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital communicators operate within a complex legal environment spanning intellectual property, privacy, advertising, defamation, and platform regulation. This course does not make you a lawyer, but it equips you to identify legal risks, ask the right questions, and avoid the common legal pitfalls that catch uninformed communicators.
key_concepts: First Amendment basics: protection of speech, commercial speech doctrine, prior restraint, and limits
Defamation: libel and slander in digital contexts, truth as defense, public figures vs. private individuals
Advertising law: FTC Act, deceptive advertising standards, substantiation requirements
Privacy law overview: tort privacy, statutory privacy (GDPR, CCPA), and the patchwork of US regulation
Platform liability: Section 230, content moderation legal framework, and pending regulatory changes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three real cases where digital communications professionals faced legal consequences: an influencer for undisclosed sponsorship, a company for misleading advertising claims, and an individual for defamation in a social media post

review.value: The First Amendment provides less protection for commercial speech (advertising, marketing) than political speech; the government can regulate commercial speech that is deceptive, misleading, or promotes illegal products under the Central Hudson test

The federal statute providing platforms immunity from liability for user-generated content posted by third parties; the foundation of social media as we know it, increasingly subject to legislative debate and potential reform

learning_objectives: Apply copyright law to digital content creation including fair use analysis
Navigate trademark law for brand naming, hashtags, and domain names
Evaluate Creative Commons and open-source licensing for content use and distribution
readings: Pember & Calvert, Mass Media Law, Ch. 12-13

lecture_notes.introduction: Every digital communicator is both a creator and a user of intellectual property. Understanding copyright protects your work and keeps you from infringing on others. Understanding trademark prevents brand conflicts. Understanding licensing enables legal use of third-party content.
key_concepts: Copyright basics: automatic protection upon creation, exclusive rights of copyright holders, registration benefits
Fair use four-factor test: purpose, nature of work, amount used, market effect
Social media and copyright: who owns content posted to platforms, reposting and embedding legality
Trademark in digital: brand names, logos, hashtags, domain names, and social media handles
Creative Commons licensing: six license types from most to least permissive, proper attribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Fair use analysis exercise: evaluating five real-world digital content uses (memes, screenshots, quotes, remixed videos, embedded tweets) against the four-factor test, discussing where each falls on the fair use spectrum

review.value: (1) Purpose and character of use (transformative? commercial?), (2) Nature of the copyrighted work (creative vs. factual), (3) Amount and substantiality used, (4) Effect on the market for the original. All four factors are weighed together; no single factor is determinative.

A system of standardized licenses allowing creators to grant specific permissions: CC BY (attribution only), CC BY-SA (share alike), CC BY-NC (non-commercial), CC BY-ND (no derivatives), and combinations; understanding these enables legal use of vast amounts of online content

learning_objectives: Apply FTC guidelines to digital advertising including social media, email, and influencer marketing
Identify what constitutes deceptive advertising and the substantiation requirements for claims
Navigate industry-specific regulations for health, financial, alcohol, and other regulated categories
readings: FTC, 'Advertising and Marketing on the Internet' (official guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every marketing claim you make must be truthful, non-deceptive, and substantiated. The FTC has increasingly focused enforcement on digital advertising, from influencer partnerships to online reviews to native advertising. Understanding these rules is not optional for digital marketers.
key_concepts: FTC Act Section 5: prohibition of unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce
Substantiation doctrine: claims must be supported by evidence before they are made, not after
Endorsement guidelines: disclosure requirements for influencers, affiliates, and employee advocates
Native advertising: FTC requirements for distinguishing paid content from editorial content
Industry-specific regulations: FDA for health claims, SEC for financial claims, TTB for alcohol
lecture_notes.demonstration: FTC compliance audit of 10 real social media posts and advertisements, identifying specific violations (undisclosed material connections, unsubstantiated claims, missing disclaimers) and recommending corrections

review.value: Advertisers must have a reasonable basis for claims before making them; the FTC can challenge any claim and demand the evidence that existed at the time the claim was made, not evidence gathered after an investigation begins

Content that resembles editorial but is paid for by an advertiser must be clearly labeled as advertising in a way that is unavoidable and unambiguous; using terms like 'sponsored content,' 'paid post,' or 'advertisement' prominently, not 'presented by' or 'in partnership with'

learning_objectives: Compare and contrast major privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, COPPA) and their requirements
Design data collection practices that comply with multiple jurisdictions simultaneously
Evaluate the impact of evolving privacy law on digital marketing techniques
readings: California Attorney General, CCPA/CPRA official FAQ

lecture_notes.introduction: Privacy law is the fastest-evolving area of digital communications regulation. What was legal last year may be illegal this year. Communications professionals must understand these laws because every campaign that collects data, every website that uses cookies, and every email list is subject to privacy regulation.
key_concepts: GDPR fundamentals: lawful basis for processing, consent requirements, data subject rights, breach notification
CCPA/CPRA: consumer rights to know, delete, and opt out of sale of personal information
COPPA: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act requirements for content and services directed at children under 13
Cookie law: ePrivacy Directive requirements and their interaction with GDPR for website tracking
Privacy by design: building privacy considerations into communications programs from the start, not as an afterthought
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a privacy compliance audit of a website: reviewing cookie consent mechanisms, privacy policy, data collection practices, email opt-in flows, and third-party data sharing, identifying violations and recommending fixes

review.value: Under GDPR, every instance of processing personal data requires a lawful basis: consent, contract performance, legal obligation, vital interests, public interest, or legitimate interest; 'because we want to' is not a lawful basis

The principle that privacy protections should be embedded into the design of systems and practices from the beginning, not added as an afterthought; for communications, this means considering data minimization, consent, and user rights at the campaign planning stage

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Legal Issues in Digital Communications
learning_objectives: Analyze pending legislation and regulatory proposals affecting digital communications
Evaluate legal implications of AI-generated content, deepfakes, and synthetic media
Develop a legal risk management framework for digital communications teams
readings: Congressional Research Service, 'Regulation of Social Media' (latest report)
Selected articles on AI-generated content legal issues (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: The law always lags behind technology. Today's most pressing legal questions in digital communications, around AI content ownership, platform regulation, algorithmic accountability, and synthetic media, are being debated in legislatures and courtrooms right now. Understanding these emerging issues prepares you for the legal landscape you will practice in.
key_concepts: AI and copyright: who owns AI-generated content, copyright registration requirements for AI-assisted works
Platform regulation proposals: content moderation mandates, algorithmic transparency, youth safety provisions
Deepfake legislation: state and federal laws emerging to address synthetic media creation and distribution
Algorithmic accountability: proposed requirements for transparency in advertising targeting and content recommendation
Legal risk management: building a framework for identifying, assessing, and mitigating legal risks in communications practice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Legal risk assessment exercise: evaluating five hypothetical communications campaigns for legal exposure, identifying specific risks (copyright, privacy, advertising, defamation), and recommending mitigation strategies

review.value: The US Copyright Office currently requires human authorship for copyright registration; purely AI-generated works cannot be copyrighted, but works where AI assisted a human creator may qualify, creating a spectrum of protection based on the level of human creative control

Proposed legislation requiring platforms to disclose how their algorithms select, rank, and recommend content to users, including advertising targeting criteria; would affect how digital communicators use platform targeting and how their content is distributed

questions.question: Explain the fair use four-factor test and apply it to this scenario: a brand creates a meme using a frame from a popular movie to promote their product on Instagram.
questions.answer: The four factors: (1) Purpose and character: commercial use (promoting a product) weighs against fair use, but if the meme is sufficiently transformative (adding new meaning or commentary), this factor is mixed. (2) Nature of the original: a Hollywood movie is a creative work, which weighs slightly against fair use. (3) Amount used: a single frame from a feature-length film is a small portion, weighing in favor. (4) Market effect: a meme does not substitute for watching the movie, weighing in favor. Overall assessment: this use is risky because the commercial purpose is clear, but the small amount used and lack of market substitution provide some protection. Many brands take this risk, but it is legally defensible only if the use is genuinely transformative rather than merely decorative.

questions.question: What is Section 230, and why is it significant for social media managers who manage user-generated content on brand social media accounts?
questions.answer: Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act provides that platforms (and by extension, those who host third-party content) are not liable for content posted by third-party users. For social media managers, this means the brand is generally not liable for defamatory, false, or harmful comments posted by users on the brand's social media pages. However, this protection has limits: if the brand creates, edits, or specifically endorses user content, it may lose Section 230 protection. Social media managers should moderate their communities but avoid editing user comments in ways that could be seen as adopting the content as their own.

rubric.key: Explains relevance to brand social media

questions.question: Explain the FTC's substantiation doctrine and describe a scenario where a digital marketing claim would violate it.
questions.answer: The substantiation doctrine requires that advertisers have a reasonable basis for claims before making them. Evidence must exist at the time the claim is made, not be gathered after a regulatory challenge. Violation scenario: a supplement brand posts on Instagram claiming their product 'boosts immune function by 300%' based on a single uncontrolled customer testimonial. This violates the substantiation doctrine because: (1) the claim is specific and quantitative, requiring scientific evidence, not testimonials, (2) a single testimonial is not reasonable substantiation for a health claim, and (3) the evidence does not exist to support the specific '300%' number.

rubric.key: Provides a specific, realistic violation scenario
Explains why the evidence is insufficient

questions.question: Compare GDPR's consent requirements with CAN-SPAM's approach to email marketing consent. Which provides stronger consumer protection, and why?
questions.answer: GDPR requires explicit, informed opt-in consent before sending marketing emails. Consent must be freely given, specific, and as easy to withdraw as to give. Pre-checked boxes are invalid. CAN-SPAM uses an opt-out model: organizations can send marketing emails to anyone until the recipient unsubscribes, requiring only that emails include an unsubscribe mechanism and a physical address. GDPR provides stronger consumer protection because: (1) it requires permission before contact rather than placing the burden on the consumer to opt out, (2) consent must be genuinely informed rather than buried in terms of service, and (3) it treats email as personal data processing requiring a lawful basis, not just a marketing channel to be regulated.

rubric.key: Explains why opt-in is stronger than opt-out

major_assignments.title: Legal Compliance Audit and Risk Framework
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive legal compliance audit of a real organization's digital communications, covering intellectual property, advertising claims, privacy practices, and platform-specific legal requirements. Develop a risk management framework with preventive measures and response protocols.
objectives: Audit digital communications for compliance with IP, advertising, privacy, and platform laws
Identify specific legal risks with severity and likelihood assessments
Develop preventive measures and training recommendations
Create response protocols for common legal issues
deliverables: Legal compliance audit report (10-12 pages) covering all major legal areas
Risk assessment matrix mapping identified risks by severity and likelihood
Legal risk management framework with preventive measures and response protocols
Training recommendations (2-3 pages) for non-legal communications staff
One-page legal quick reference guide for daily content creation decisions

criteria: Audit covers IP, advertising, privacy, and platform compliance
Specific issues are identified with legal citations
Assessment distinguishes between high-risk and low-risk issues
Competitive context is considered
Risk matrix is properly structured with severity and likelihood
Risks are specific to the organization, not generic
Prioritization is logical and justified
Emerging legal risks are considered
Preventive measures are practical and implementable
Response protocols cover common scenarios
Training recommendations are appropriate for non-legal staff
Framework is sustainable and maintainable
Quick reference is genuinely usable in daily work
Covers the most common legal questions content creators face
Language is accessible to non-lawyers
Decision trees or checklists are clear and practical
Thorough legal audit demonstrating knowledge across all major areas
Rigorous risk analysis with organization-specific assessment
Practical risk management framework implementable by non-lawyers
Useful quick reference guide for daily content creation

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on legal concepts affecting digital communications

weekly_case_analyses.description: Weekly legal case studies and compliance exercises

major_assignment.description: Legal compliance audit and risk management framework

midterm_examination.description: Written examination on IP, advertising law, and privacy regulation

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination with live legal risk assessment exercise

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Legal analysis requires discussion and participation. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Legal audit components must be submitted on time for peer review.
course_policies.collaboration: Case analysis discussions are collaborative. All written deliverables are individual. Sharing audit documents is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Legal citations must be accurate. Case analysis must reflect genuine understanding. Copying legal analysis from online sources constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need internet access for legal research and a laptop for in-class analysis. Legal databases are accessed through university library subscriptions.
